content.text: 现在让我读取 `netlist` 基类和 `ClusteredNetlist` 的实现，以了解其细节。

content.content:
1	#pragma once
2	/**
3	 * @file
4	 * @brief This file defines the Netlist class, which stores the connectivity information
5	 * of the components in a netlist. It is the base class for AtomNetlist and ClusteredNetlist.
6	 *
7	 * Overview
8	 * ========
9	 * The netlist logically consists of several different components: Blocks, Ports, Pins and Nets
10	 * Each component in the netlist has a unique template identifier (BlockId, PortId, PinId, NetId)
11	 * used to retrieve information about it. In this implementation these ID's are unique throughout the
12	 * netlist (i.e. every port in the netlist has a unique ID, even if the ports share a common type).
13	 *
14	 * Block
15	 * -----
16	 * A Block is the primitive netlist element (a node in the netlist hyper-graph).
17	 * Blocks have various attributes (a name, a type etc.) and are associated with sets of
18	 * input/output/clock ports.
19	 *
20	 * Block related information can be retrieved using the block_*() member functions.
21	 *
22	 * Pins
23	 * ----
24	 * Pins define single-bit connections between a block and a net.
25	 *
26	 * Pin related information can be retrieved using the pin_*() member functions.
27	 *
28	 * Nets
29	 * ----
30	 * Nets represent the connections between blocks (the edges of the netlist hyper-graph).
31	 * Each net has a single driver pin, and a set of sink pins.
32	 *
33	 * Net related information can be retrieved using the net_*() member functions.
34	 *
35	 * Ports
36	 * -----
37	 * A Port is a (potentially multi-bit) group of pins.
38	 *
39	 * For example, the two operands and output of an N-bit adder would logically be grouped as three ports.
40	 * Ports have a specified bit-width which defines how many pins form the port.
41	 *
42	 * Port related information can be retrieved using the port_*() member functions.
43	 *
44	 *
45	 * Usage
46	 * =====
47	 *
48	 * The following provides usage examples for common use-cases.
49	 *
50	 * Walking the netlist
51	 * -------------------
52	 * To iterate over the whole netlist use the blocks() and/or nets() member functions:
53	 *
54	 *      Netlist netlist;
55	 *
56	 *      //... initialize the netlist
57	 *
58	 *      //Iterate over all the blocks
59	 *      for(BlockId blk_id : netlist.blocks()) {
60	 *          //Do something with each block
61	 *      }
62	 *
63	 *
64	 *      //Iterate over all the nets
65	 *      for(NetId net_id : netlist.nets()) {
66	 *          //Do something with each net
67	 *      }
68	 *
69	 * To retrieve information about a netlist component call one of the associated member functions:
70	 *
71	 *      //Print out each block's name
72	 *      for(BlockId blk_id : netlist.blocks()) {
73	 *
74	 *          //Get the block name
75	 *          const std::string& block_name = netlist.block_name(blk_id);
76	 *
77	 *          //Print it
78	 *          printf("Block: %s\n", block_name.c_str());
79	 *      }
80	 *
81	 * Note that the member functions are associated with the type of component (e.g. block_name() yields
82	 * the name of a block, net_name() yields the name of a net).
83	 *
84	 * Tracing cross-references
85	 * ------------------------
86	 * It is common to need to trace the netlist connectivity. The Netlist allows this to be done
87	 * efficiently by maintaining cross-references between the various netlist components.
88	 *
89	 * The following diagram shows the main methods and relationships between netlist components:
90	 * ```
91	 *              +---------+      pin_block()
92	 *              |         |<--------------------------+
93	 *              |  Block  |                           |
94	 *              |         |-----------------------+   |
95	 *              +---------+      block_pins()     |   |
96	 *                 |   ^                          v   |
97	 *                 |   |                       +---------+  net_pins()  +---------+
98	 *                 |   |                       |         |<-------------|         |
99	 *   block_ports() |   | port_block()          |   Pin   |              |   Net   |
100	 *                 |   |                       |         |------------->|         |
101	 *                 |   |                       +---------+  pin_net()   +---------+
102	 *                 v   |                          ^   |
103	 *              +---------+      port_pins()      |   |
104	 *              |         |-----------------------+   |
105	 *              |  Port   |                           |
106	 *              |         |<--------------------------+
107	 *              +---------+      pin_port()
108	 * ```
109	 * <!-- NOTE: Markdown ``` are used to display the code properly in the documentation -->
110	 *
111	 * Note that methods which are plurals (e.g. net_pins()) return multiple components.
112	 *
113	 * As an example consider the case where we wish to find all the blocks associated with a particular net:
114	 *
115	 *      NetId net_id;
116	 *
117	 *      //... Initialize net_id with the net of interest
118	 *
119	 *      //Iterate through each pin on the net to get the associated port
120	 *      for(PinId pin_id : netlist.net_pins(net_id)) {
121	 *
122	 *          //Get the port associated with the pin
123	 *          PortId port_id = netlist.pin_port(pin_id);
124	 *
125	 *          //Get the block associated with the port
126	 *          BlockId blk_id = netlist.port_block(port_id);
127	 *
128	 *          //Print out the block name
129	 *          const std::string& block_name = netlist.block_name(blk_id);
130	 *          printf("Associated block: %s\n", block_name.c_str());
131	 *      }
132	 *
133	 * Netlist also defines some convenience functions for common operations to avoid tracking the intermediate IDs
134	 * if they are not needed. The following produces the same result as above:
135	 *
136	 *      NetId net_id;
137	 *
138	 *      //... Initialize net_id with the net of interest
139	 *
140	 *      //Iterate through each pin on the net to get the associated port
141	 *      for(PinId pin_id : netlist.net_pins(net_id)) {
142	 *
143	 *          //Get the block associated with the pin (bypassing the port)
144	 *          BlockId blk_id = netlist.pin_block(pin_id);
145	 *
146	 *          //Print out the block name
147	 *          const std::string& block_name = netlist.block_name(blk_id);
148	 *          printf("Associated block: %s\n", block_name.c_str());
149	 *      }
150	 *
151	 *
152	 * As another example, consider the inverse problem of identifying the nets connected as inputs to a particular block:
153	 *
154	 *      BlkId blk_id;
155	 *
156	 *      //... Initialize blk_id with the block of interest
157	 *
158	 *      //Iterate through the ports
159	 *      for(PortId port_id : netlist.block_input_ports(blk_id)) {
160	 *
161	 *          //Iterate through the pins
162	 *          for(PinId pin_id : netlist.port_pins(port_id)) {
163	 *              //Retrieve the net
164	 *              NetId net_id = netlist.pin_net(pin_id);
165	 *
166	 *              //Get its name
167	 *              const std::string& net_name = netlist.net_name(net_id);
168	 *              printf("Associated net: %s\n", net_name.c_str());
169	 *          }
170	 *      }
171	 *
172	 * Here we used the block_input_ports() method which returned an iterable range of all the input ports
173	 * associated with blk_id. We then used the port_pins() method to get iterable ranges of all the pins
174	 * associated with each port, from which we can find the associated net.
175	 *
176	 * Often port information is not relevant so this can be further simplified by iterating over a block's pins
177	 * directly (e.g. by calling one of the block_*_pins() functions):
178	 *
179	 *      BlkId blk_id;
180	 *
181	 *      //... Initialize blk_id with the block of interest
182	 *
183	 *      //Iterate over the blocks ports directly
184	 *      for(PinId pin_id : netlist.block_input_pins(blk_id)) {
185	 *
186	 *          //Retrieve the net
187	 *          NetId net_id = netlist.pin_net(pin_id);
188	 *
189	 *          //Get its name
190	 *          const std::string& net_name = netlist.net_name(net_id);
191	 *          printf("Associated net: %s\n", net_name.c_str());
192	 *      }
193	 *
194	 * Note the use of range-based-for loops in the above examples; it could also have written (more verbosely)
195	 * using a conventional for loop and explicit iterators as follows:
196	 *
197	 *      BlkId blk_id;
198	 *
199	 *      //... Initialize blk_id with the block of interest
200	 *
201	 *      //Iterate over the blocks ports directly
202	 *      auto pins = netlist.block_input_pins(blk_id);
203	 *      for(auto pin_iter = pins.begin(); pin_iter != pins.end(); ++pin_iter) {
204	 *
205	 *          //Retrieve the net
206	 *          NetId net_id = netlist.pin_net(*pin_iter);
207	 *
208	 *          //Get its name
209	 *          const std::string& net_name = netlist.net_name(net_id);
210	 *          printf("Associated net: %s\n", net_name.c_str());
211	 *      }
212	 *
213	 *
214	 * Creating the netlist
215	 * --------------------
216	 * The netlist can be created by using the create_*() member functions to create individual Blocks/Ports/Pins/Nets.
217	 *
218	 * For instance to create the following netlist (where each block is the same type, and has an input port 'A'
219	 * and output port 'B'):
220	 *
221	 *      -----------        net1         -----------
222	 *      | block_1 |-------------------->| block_2 |
223	 *      -----------          |          -----------
224	 *                           |
225	 *                           |          -----------
226	 *                           ---------->| block_3 |
227	 *                                      -----------
228	 * We could do the following:
229	 *
230	 *      const t_model* blk_model = .... //Initialize the block model appropriately
231	 *
232	 *      Netlist netlist("my_netlist"); //Initialize the netlist with name 'my_netlist'
233	 *
234	 *      //Create the first block
235	 *      BlockId blk1 = netlist.create_block("block_1", blk_model);
236	 *
237	 *      //Create the first block's output port
238	 *      //  Note that the input/output/clock type of the port is determined
239	 *      //  automatically from the block model
240	 *      PortId blk1_out = netlist.create_port(blk1, "B");
241	 *
242	 *      //Create the net
243	 *      NetId net1 = netlist.create_net("net1");
244	 *
245	 *      //Associate the net with blk1
246	 *      netlist.create_pin(blk1_out, 0, net1, PinType::DRIVER);
247	 *
248	 *      //Create block 2 and hook it up to net1
249	 *      BlockId blk2 = netlist.create_block("block_2", blk_model);
250	 *      PortId blk2_in = netlist.create_port(blk2, "A");
251	 *      netlist.create_pin(blk2_in, 0, net1, PinType::SINK);
252	 *
253	 *      //Create block 3 and hook it up to net1
254	 *      BlockId blk3 = netlist.create_block("block_3", blk_model);
255	 *      PortId blk3_in = netlist.create_port(blk3, "A");
256	 *      netlist.create_pin(blk3_in, 0, net1, PinType::SINK);
257	 *
258	 * Modifying the netlist
259	 * ---------------------
260	 * The netlist can also be modified by using the remove_*() member functions. If we wanted to remove
261	 * block_3 from the netlist creation example above we could do the following:
262	 *
263	 *      //Mark blk3 and any references to it invalid
264	 *      netlist.remove_block(blk3);
265	 *
266	 *      //Compress the netlist to actually remove the data associated with blk3
267	 *      // NOTE: This will invalidate all client held IDs (e.g. blk1, blk1_out, net1, blk2, blk2_in)
268	 *      netlist.compress();
269	 *
270	 * The resulting netlist connectivity now looks like:
271	 *
272	 *      -----------        net1         -----------
273	 *      | block_1 |-------------------->| block_2 |
274	 *      -----------                     -----------
275	 *
276	 * Note that until compress() is called any 'removed' elements will have invalid IDs (e.g. BlockId::INVALID()).
277	 * As a result after calling remove_block() (which invalidates blk3) we *then* called compress() to remove the
278	 * invalid IDs.
279	 *
280	 * Also note that compress() is relatively slow. As a result avoid calling compress() after every call to
281	 * a remove_*() function, and instead batch up calls to remove_*() and call compress() only after a set of
282	 * modifications have been applied.
283	 *
284	 * Verifying the netlist
285	 * ---------------------
286	 * Particularly after construction and/or modification it is a good idea to check that the netlist is in
287	 * a valid and consistent state. This can be done with the verify() member function:
288	 *
289	 *      netlist.verify()
290	 *
291	 * If the netlist is not valid verify() will throw an exception, otherwise it returns true.
292	 *
293	 * Invariants
294	 * ==========
295	 * The Netlist maintains stronger invariants if the netlist is in compressed form.
296	 *
297	 * Netlist is compressed ('not dirty')
298	 * -----------------------------------
299	 * If the netlist is compressed (i.e. !is_dirty(), meaning there have been NO calls to remove_*() since the last call to
300	 * compress()) the following invariant will hold:
301	 *      - Any range returned will contain only valid IDs
302	 *
303	 * In practise this means the following conditions hold:
304	 *      - Blocks will not contain empty ports/pins (e.g. ports with no pin/net connections)
305	 *      - Ports will not contain pins with no associated net
306	 *      - Nets will not contain invalid sink pins
307	 *
308	 * This means that no error checking for invalid IDs is needed if simply iterating through netlist (see below for
309	 * some exceptions).
310	 *
311	 * NOTE: you may still encounter invalid IDs in the following cases:
312	 *      - net_driver() will return an invalid ID if the net is undriven
313	 *      - port_pin()/port_net() will return an invalid ID if the bit index corresponds to an unconnected pin
314	 *
315	 * Netlist is NOT compressed ('dirty')
316	 * -----------------------------------
317	 * If the netlist is not compressed (i.e. is_dirty(), meaning there have been calls to remove_*() with no subsequent
318	 * calls to compress()) then the invariant above does not hold.
319	 *
320	 * Any range may return invalid IDs. In practise this means,
321	 *      - Blocks may contain invalid ports/pins
322	 *      - Ports may contain invalid pins
323	 *      - Pins may not have a valid associated net
324	 *      - Nets may contain invalid sink pins
325	 *
326	 * Implementation Details
327	 * ======================
328	 * The netlist is stored in Struct-of-Arrays format rather than the more conventional Array-of-Structs.
329	 * This improves cache locality by keeping component attributes of the same type in contiguous memory.
330	 * This prevents unneeded member data from being pulled into the cache (since most code accesses only a few
331	 * attributes at a time this tends to be more efficient).
332	 *
333	 * Clients of this class pass nearly-opaque IDs (BlockId, PortId, PinId, NetId, StringId) to retrieve
334	 * information. The ID is internally converted to an index to retrieve the required value from it's associated storage.
335	 *
336	 * By using nearly-opaque IDs we can change the underlying data layout as need to optimize performance/memory, without
337	 * disrupting client code.
338	 *
339	 * Strings
340	 * -------
341	 * To minimize memory usage, we store each unique string only once in the netlist and give it a unique ID (StringId).
342	 * Any references to this string then make use of the StringId.
343	 *
344	 * In particular this prevents the (potentially large) strings from begin duplicated multiple times in various look-ups,
345	 * instead the more space efficient StringId is duplicated.
346	 *
347	 * Note that StringId is an internal implementation detail and should not be exposed as part of the public interface.
348	 * Any public functions should take and return std::string's instead.
349	 *
350	 * Block pins/Block ports data layout
351	 * ----------------------------------
352	 * The pins/ports for each block are stored in a similar manner, for brevity we describe only pins here.
353	 *
354	 * The pins for each block (i.e. PinId's) are stored in a single vector for each block (the block_pins_ member).
355	 * This allows us to iterate over all pins (i.e. block_pins()), or specific subsets of pins (e.g. only inputs with
356	 * block_input_pins()).
357	 *
358	 * To accomplish this all pins of the same group (input/output/clock) are located next to each other. An example is
359	 * shown below, where the block has n input pins, m output pins and k clock pins.
360	 *
361	 * ```
362	 *  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
363	 *  | ipin_1 | ipin_2 | ... | ipin_n | opin_1 | opin_2 | ... | opin_m | clock_pin_1 | clock_pin_2 | ... | clock_pin_k |
364	 *  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
365	 *  ^                                ^                                ^                                               ^
366	 *  |                                |                                |                                               |
367	 * begin                         opin_begin                    clock_pin_begin                                       end
368	 * ```
369	 *
370	 * Provided we know the internal dividing points (i.e. opin_begin and clock_pin_begin) we can easily build the various
371	 * ranges of interest:
372	 *
373	 *      all pins   : [begin, end)
374	 *      input pins : [begin, opin_begin)
375	 *      output pins: [opin_begin, clock_pin_begin)
376	 *      clock pins : [clock_pin_begin, end)
377	 *
378	 * Since any reallocation would invalidate any iterators to these internal dividers, we separately store the number
379	 * of input/output/clock pins per block (i.e. in block_num_input_pins_, block_num_output_pins_ and
380	 * block_num_clock_pins_). The internal dividers can then be easily calculated (e.g. see block_output_pins()), even
381	 * if new pins are inserted (provided the counts are updated).
382	 *
383	 * Adding data to the netlist
384	 * --------------------------
385	 * The Netlist should contain only information directly related to the netlist state (i.e. netlist connectivity).
386	 * Various mappings to/from elements (e.g. what CLB contains an atom block), and algorithmic state (e.g. if a net
387	 * is routed) do NOT constitute netlist state and should NOT be stored here.
388	 *
389	 * Such implementation state should be stored in other data structures (which may reference the Netlist's IDs).
390	 *
391	 * The netlist state should be immutable (i.e. read-only) for most of the CAD flow.
392	 *
393	 * Interactions with other netlists
394	 * --------------------------------
395	 * Currently, the AtomNetlist and ClusteredNetlist are both derived from Netlist. The AtomNetlist has primitive
396	 * specific details (t_model, TruthTable), and handles all operations with the atoms. The ClusteredNetlist
397	 * contains information on the CLB (Clustered Logic Block) level, which includes the physical description of the
398	 * blocks (t_logical_block_type), as well as the internal hierarchy and wiring (t_pb/t_pb_route).
399	 *
400	 * The calling-conventions of the functions in the AtomNetlist and ClusteredNetlist is as follows:
401	 *
402	 *    Functions where the derived class (Atom/Clustered) calls the base class (Netlist)
403	 *       create_*()
404	 *
405	 *    Functions where the base class calls the derived class (Non-Virtual Interface idiom as described
406	 *    https://en.wikibooks.org/wiki/More_C%2B%2B_Idioms/Non-Virtual_Interface)
407	 *       remove_*()
408	 *       clean_*()
409	 *       validate_*_sizes()
410	 *       shrink_to_fit()
411	 *    The derived functions based off of the virtual functions have suffix *_impl()
412	 *
413	 */
414	#include <string>
415	#include <vector>
416	#include <unordered_map>
417	#include "vtr_range.h"
418	#include "vtr_vector_map.h"
419	
420	#include "netlist_fwd.h"
421	
422	//Forward declaration for private methods
423	template<typename BlockId, typename PortId, typename PinId, typename NetId>
424	class NetlistIdRemapper {
425	  public:
426	    BlockId new_block_id(BlockId old_blk) const;
427	    PortId new_port_id(PortId old_port) const;
428	    PinId new_pin_id(PinId old_pin) const;
429	    NetId new_net_id(NetId old_net) const;
430	
431	  private:
432	    friend Netlist<BlockId, PortId, PinId, NetId>;
433	
434	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, BlockId> block_id_map_;
435	    vtr::vector_map<PortId, PortId> port_id_map_;
436	    vtr::vector_map<PinId, PinId> pin_id_map_;
437	    vtr::vector_map<NetId, NetId> net_id_map_;
438	};
439	
440	template<typename BlockId = ParentBlockId, typename PortId = ParentPortId, typename PinId = ParentPinId, typename NetId = ParentNetId>
441	class Netlist {
442	  public: //Public Types
443	    typedef typename vtr::vector_map<BlockId, BlockId>::const_iterator block_iterator;
444	    typedef typename std::unordered_map<std::string, std::string>::const_iterator attr_iterator;
445	    typedef typename std::unordered_map<std::string, std::string>::const_iterator param_iterator;
446	    typedef typename vtr::vector_map<NetId, NetId>::const_iterator net_iterator;
447	    typedef typename vtr::vector_map<PinId, PinId>::const_iterator pin_iterator;
448	    typedef typename vtr::vector_map<PortId, PortId>::const_iterator port_iterator;
449	
450	    typedef typename vtr::Range<block_iterator> block_range;
451	    typedef typename vtr::Range<attr_iterator> attr_range;
452	    typedef typename vtr::Range<param_iterator> param_range;
453	    typedef typename vtr::Range<net_iterator> net_range;

728	    BlockId find_block(const std::string& name) const;

730	    /**
731	     * @brief Finds a block where the block's name contains the
732	     *        provided input name as a substring. 
733	     *        The intended use is to find the block id of a 
734	     *        hard block without knowing its name in the netlist. Instead
735	     *        the block's module name in the HDL design can be used as it will
736	     *        be a substring within its full name in the netlist.
737	     * 
738	     *        For example, suppose a RAM block was named in the netlist as
739	     *        "top|alu|test_ram|out". The user instantiated the ram module
740	     *        in the HDL design as "test_ram". So instead of going through 
741	     *        the netlist and finding the ram block's full name, this
742	     *        function can be used by just providing the module name "test_ram"
743	     *        and using this substring to match the blocks name in the netlist
744	     *        and retrieving its block id. If no blocks matched to input pattern
745	     *        then an invalid block id is returned.
746	     * 
747	     *        This function runs in linear time (O(N)) as it goes over all the 
748	     *        cluster blocks in the netlist. Additionally, if there are multiple
749	     *        blocks that contain the provided input as a substring, then the
750	     *        first block found is returned.
751	     * 
752	     *        NOTE: This function tries to find blocks by checking for 
753	     *              substrings.
754	     *              The clustered netlist class defines another version of this
755	     *              function that find blocks by checking for a pattern match,
756	     *              meaning that the input is a pattern string and the pattern
757	     *              is looked for in each block name.
758	     * 
759	     * @param name_substring A substring of a block name for which an ID needs  
760	     *             to be found.
761	     * @return A cluster block id representing a unique cluster block that 
762	     *         matched to the input string pattern.
763	     *        
764	     */
765	    BlockId find_block_by_name_fragment(const std::string& name_substring) const;
766	
767	    /**
768	     * @brief Returns the PortId of the specified port if it exists or PortId::INVALID() if not
769	     *
770	     * @note  This method is typically less efficient than searching by a t_model_port
771	     *        With the overloaded AtomNetlist method
772	     *   @param blk_id   The ID of the block who's ports will be checked
773	     *   @param name     The name of the port to look for
774	     */
775	    PortId find_port(const BlockId blk_id, const std::string& name) const;
776	
777	    /**

1042	    /*
1043	     * Sanity Checks
1044	     */
1045	
1046	    //Verify the internal data structure sizes match
1047	    bool verify_sizes() const;
1048	    bool validate_block_sizes() const;
1049	    bool validate_port_sizes() const;
1050	    bool validate_pin_sizes() const;
1051	    bool validate_net_sizes() const;
1052	    bool validate_string_sizes() const;
1053	
1054	    //Verify that internal data structure cross-references are consistent
1055	    bool verify_refs() const; //All cross-references
1056	    bool validate_block_port_refs() const;
1057	    bool validate_block_pin_refs() const;
1058	    bool validate_port_pin_refs() const;
1059	    bool validate_net_pin_refs() const;
1060	    bool validate_string_refs() const;
1061	
1062	    ///@brief Verify that block invariants hold (i.e. logical consistency)
1063	    bool verify_block_invariants() const;
1064	
1065	    ///@brief Verify that fast-lookups are consistent with internal data structures
1066	    bool verify_lookups() const;
1067	
1068	    ///@brief Validates that the specified ID is valid in the current netlist state
1069	    bool valid_string_id(StringId string_id) const;
1070	
1071	  protected: //Protected virtual functions implemented in derived classes
1072	    //The functions follow the Non-Virtual Interface (NVI) idiom, and
1073	    //are called from this class in their respective non-impl() functions.
1074	    virtual void shrink_to_fit_impl() {}
1075	
1076	    virtual bool validate_block_sizes_impl(size_t /*num_blocks*/) const { return true; }
1077	    virtual bool validate_port_sizes_impl(size_t /*num_ports*/) const { return true; }
1078	    virtual bool validate_pin_sizes_impl(size_t /*num_pins*/) const { return true; }
1079	    virtual bool validate_net_sizes_impl(size_t /*num_nets*/) const { return true; }
1080	
1081	    virtual void clean_blocks_impl(const vtr::vector_map<BlockId, BlockId>& /*block_id_map*/) {}
1082	    virtual void clean_ports_impl(const vtr::vector_map<PortId, PortId>& /*port_id_map*/) {}
1083	    virtual void clean_pins_impl(const vtr::vector_map<PinId, PinId>& /*pin_id_map*/) {}
1084	    virtual void clean_nets_impl(const vtr::vector_map<NetId, NetId>& /*net_id_map*/) {}
1085	
1086	    virtual void remove_block_impl(const BlockId /*blk_id*/) {}
1087	    virtual void remove_port_impl(const PortId /*port_id*/) {}
1088	    virtual void remove_pin_impl(const PinId /*pin_id*/) {}
1089	    virtual void remove_net_impl(const NetId /*net_id*/) {}
1090	
1091	    virtual void rebuild_block_refs_impl(const vtr::vector_map<PinId, PinId>& /*pin_id_map*/, const vtr::vector_map<PortId, PortId>& /*port_id_map*/) {}
1092	    virtual void rebuild_port_refs_impl(const vtr::vector_map<BlockId, BlockId>& /*block_id_map*/, const vtr::vector_map<PinId, PinId>& /*pin_id_map*/) {}
1093	    virtual void rebuild_pin_refs_impl(const vtr::vector_map<PortId, PortId>& /*port_id_map*/, const vtr::vector_map<NetId, NetId>& /*net_id_map*/) {}
1094	    virtual void rebuild_net_refs_impl(const vtr::vector_map<PinId, PinId>& /*pin_id_map*/) {}
1095	
1096	  protected:
1097	    constexpr static int INVALID_INDEX = -1;
1098	    constexpr static int NET_DRIVER_INDEX = 0;
1099	
1100	  private:                     //Data
1101	    std::string netlist_name_; ///<Name of the top-level netlist
1102	    std::string netlist_id_;   ///<Unique identifier for the netlist
1103	    bool dirty_ = false;       ///<Indicates the netlist has invalid entries from remove_*() functions
1104	
1105	    //Block data
1106	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, BlockId> block_ids_;    ///<Valid block ids
1107	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, StringId> block_names_; ///<Name of each block
1108	
1109	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, std::vector<PortId>> block_ports_; ///<Ports of each block
1110	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, unsigned> block_num_input_ports_;  ///<Input ports of each block
1111	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, unsigned> block_num_output_ports_; ///<Output ports of each block
1112	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, unsigned> block_num_clock_ports_;  ///<Clock ports of each block
1113	
1114	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, std::vector<PinId>> block_pins_;  ///<Pins of each block
1115	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, unsigned> block_num_input_pins_;  ///<Number of input pins on each block
1116	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, unsigned> block_num_output_pins_; ///<Number of output pins on each block
1117	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, unsigned> block_num_clock_pins_;  ///<Number of clock pins on each block
1118	
1119	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, std::unordered_map<std::string, std::string>> block_params_; ///<Parameters of each block
1120	    vtr::vector_map<BlockId, std::unordered_map<std::string, std::string>> block_attrs_;  ///<Attributes of each block
1121	
1122	    //Port data
1123	    vtr::vector_map<PortId, PortId> port_ids_;              ///<Valid port ids
1124	    vtr::vector_map<PortId, StringId> port_names_;          ///<Name of each port
1125	    vtr::vector_map<PortId, BlockId> port_blocks_;          ///<Block associated with each port
1126	    vtr::vector_map<PortId, std::vector<PinId>> port_pins_; ///<Pins associated with each port
1127	    vtr::vector_map<PortId, BitIndex> port_widths_;         ///<Width (in bits) of each port
1128	    vtr::vector_map<PortId, PortType> port_types_;          ///<Type of the port (INPUT, OUTPUT, CLOCK)
1129	
1130	    //Pin data
1131	    vtr::vector_map<PinId, PinId> pin_ids_;          ///<Valid pin ids
1132	    vtr::vector_map<PinId, PortId> pin_ports_;       ///<Type of each pin
1133	    vtr::vector_map<PinId, BitIndex> pin_port_bits_; ///<The pins bit position in the port
1134	    vtr::vector_map<PinId, NetId> pin_nets_;         ///<Net associated with each pin
1135	    vtr::vector_map<PinId, int> pin_net_indices_;    ///<Index of the specified pin within it's associated net
1136	    vtr::vector_map<PinId, bool> pin_is_constant_;   ///<Indicates if the pin always keeps a constant value
1137	
1138	    //Net data
1139	    vtr::vector_map<NetId, NetId> net_ids_;               ///<Valid net ids
1140	    vtr::vector_map<NetId, StringId> net_names_;          ///<Name of each net
1141	    vtr::vector_map<NetId, std::vector<PinId>> net_pins_; ///<Pins associated with each net
1142	
1143	    //String data
1144	    // We store each unique string once, and reference it by an StringId
1145	    // This avoids duplicating the strings in the fast look-ups (i.e. the look-ups
1146	    // only store the Ids)
1147	    vtr::vector_map<StringId, StringId> string_ids_; ///<Valid string ids
1148	    vtr::vector_map<StringId, std::string> strings_; ///<Strings
1149	
1150	  private: //Fast lookups
1151	    vtr::vector_map<StringId, BlockId> block_name_to_block_id_;
1152	    vtr::vector_map<StringId, NetId> net_name_to_net_id_;
1153	    std::unordered_map<std::string, StringId> string_to_string_id_;
1154	    vtr::vector_map<NetId, bool> net_is_ignored_; ///<Boolean mapping indicating if the net is ignored
1155	    vtr::vector_map<NetId, bool> net_is_global_;  ///<Boolean mapping indicating if the net is global
1156	};
